nodes.name: Countries of the World
nodes.description: Country sets by region and category

config.prompt: Name all 45 sovereign countries of Europe

items.explanation:
Located on the western Balkan Peninsula along the Adriatic and Ionian Seas; capital is Tirana
One of Europe's most isolated countries during communism (1944–1992) under Enver Hoxha, who banned religion nationwide
More than half the population emigrated abroad after the regime fell — one of the largest post-communist diasporas relative to population size

Microstate in the Pyrenees between France and Spain; capital is Andorra la Vella
Andorra la Vella is the highest capital city in Europe at ~1,023 m elevation
One of the smallest countries in Europe (~468 km²); relies on tourism and duty-free shopping; technically a co-principality ruled jointly by the President of France and the Bishop of Urgell

Landlocked country in Central Europe; capital is Vienna (Wien)
Center of the Habsburg Empire for centuries; Austria-Hungary was one of the great powers dissolved after WWI
Vienna is famous for its classical music legacy — Haydn, Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Brahms, and Mahler all lived and worked there

Landlocked country in Eastern Europe bordering Russia, Ukraine, Poland, Lithuania, and Latvia; capital is Minsk
Often called 'Europe's last dictatorship' — Alexander Lukashenko has ruled since 1994
Minsk was almost completely destroyed in WWII and rebuilt as a model Soviet city; it contains one of the largest WWII memorial complexes in the world

Small country at the crossroads of Western Europe; capital is Brussels, which also serves as de facto capital of the European Union and NATO headquarters
Deeply divided between Dutch-speaking Flanders in the north and French-speaking Wallonia in the south; went 541 days without a government in 2010–2011, a world record
Birthplace of the saxophone (Adolphe Sax, 1814) and famous for waffles, frites, and over 1,500 beer varieties

Located in the western Balkans; capital is Sarajevo
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo in 1914 triggered WWI
Suffered a devastating war (1992–1995) during the breakup of Yugoslavia; the Siege of Sarajevo lasted nearly 4 years — the longest siege of a capital city in modern warfare

Located in southeastern Europe on the Black Sea; capital is Sofia
One of the oldest states in Europe — the First Bulgarian Empire was founded in 681 CE
The Cyrillic alphabet was developed in the 9th century by Saints Cyril and Methodius, who were from the region of modern Bulgaria; Bulgaria is known for Cyrillic as part of its cultural legacy

Located on the Adriatic Sea in the western Balkans; capital is Zagreb
Declared independence from Yugoslavia in 1991; the Dalmatian Coast and Dubrovnik are among Europe's most visited destinations
The tie (cravat) originates from Croatian soldiers who wore distinctive neck scarves — 'cravat' derives from 'Croat'

Island nation in the northeastern Mediterranean; capital is Nicosia
Divided since 1974 when Turkey invaded the north — the island is split between the Republic of Cyprus (Greek Cypriot, EU member) and the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus (recognized only by Turkey)
Nicosia is the last divided capital city in the world

Landlocked country in Central Europe; capital is Prague
Formed in 1993 when Czechoslovakia peacefully split into the Czech Republic and Slovakia — the 'Velvet Divorce'
Prague's historic center, including its medieval Old Town and Astronomical Clock (1410), is a UNESCO World Heritage Site

Scandinavian country on the Jutland Peninsula and numerous islands; capital is Copenhagen
The oldest kingdom in Europe — the Danish monarchy dates back over 1,000 years; it also governs Greenland, the world's largest island
LEGO was invented in Denmark in 1932; the word comes from the Danish 'leg godt' meaning 'play well'

Smallest of the three Baltic states; capital is Tallinn
Occupied by the Soviet Union 1940–1991; declared independence during the 1991 coup attempt and was recognized within days
The Tallinn Old Town is a UNESCO World Heritage Site; Estonia is one of the most digitally advanced societies in the world — the first country to offer e-residency and conduct binding elections online

Nordic country in Northern Europe bordering Russia, Sweden, and Norway; capital is Helsinki
Declared independence from Russia in 1917; fought the Winter War (1939–1940) against the Soviet Union and retained independence despite massive losses
Has the highest number of heavy metal bands per capita of any country; also famous for sauna culture — there are more saunas than cars in Finland

Located in Western Europe; capital is Paris, the largest city in Western Europe
The world's most visited country, receiving ~90 million tourists annually; home to the Louvre (world's most visited art museum)
The French Revolution (1789) established principles of liberty, equality, and fraternity that shaped modern democratic governance worldwide
Napoleon Bonaparte built a French empire across most of Europe before his defeat at Waterloo (1815)

Located in Central Europe; capital is Berlin, the largest city in the EU
Divided into East and West Germany 1945–1990; the Berlin Wall fell November 9, 1989; reunification followed October 3, 1990
Largest economy in Europe and the world's third or fourth largest; the Rhine and Ruhr valleys were the industrial core of 20th-century Europe
Birthplace of the Protestant Reformation (Martin Luther, 1517) and the printing press (Gutenberg, c. 1440)

Located in southeastern Europe on the Mediterranean; capital is Athens
Birthplace of democracy, Western philosophy, and the Olympic Games; the Parthenon (447–432 BCE) is the most famous ancient monument in Europe
Comprises thousands of islands including Crete (largest), Rhodes, and Santorini; joined the EU in 1981
Hosted the first modern Olympics (1896) and the 2004 Athens Olympics

Landlocked country in Central Europe; capital is Budapest
Budapest was formed in 1873 by merging Buda, Pest, and Óbuda across the Danube; its Parliament Building is one of the world's largest legislative buildings
Hungarian (Magyar) is unrelated to most European languages — it belongs to the Finno-Ugric family along with Finnish and Estonian; famous for goulash and the Rubik's Cube (invented by Ernő Rubik, 1974)

Island nation in the North Atlantic near the Arctic Circle; capital is Reykjavik, the world's northernmost capital of a sovereign state
Volcanically active — sits atop the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where two tectonic plates meet; home to geysers (the word 'geyser' comes from the Icelandic Geysir)
Settled by Norse Vikings c. 874 CE; the Althing (established 930 CE) is the world's oldest surviving parliament

Island nation in the North Atlantic west of Great Britain; capital is Dublin
Famine of 1845–1852 reduced the population by ~25% through death and emigration; the Irish diaspora is massive — more people of Irish descent live in the U.S. than in Ireland itself
Joined the EU in 1973; the Celtic Tiger economic boom (1990s–2008) transformed it from one of Europe's poorest to one of its wealthiest countries per capita

Boot-shaped peninsula in Southern Europe extending into the Mediterranean; capital is Rome
Contains two independent microstates: Vatican City and San Marino — the only country to have two other countries within its borders
Center of the Roman Empire and the Renaissance; has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country (~58)
Founded as a unified state in 1861; Mussolini led the fascist government 1922–1943

Middle of the three Baltic states; capital is Riga
Riga's historic center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site known for its Art Nouveau architecture — it has the highest concentration of Art Nouveau buildings of any city in the world
Occupied by the Soviet Union 1940–1991; regained independence in 1991; joined NATO and the EU in 2004

One of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world (the other is Uzbekistan); borders only Switzerland and Austria; capital is Vaduz
Smallest German-speaking country; sixth-smallest country in the world by area (~160 km²)
Relies heavily on financial services and low corporate taxes; has one of the highest GDPs per capita in the world

Largest of the three Baltic states; capital is Vilnius
The Grand Duchy of Lithuania was once the largest state in Europe (14th–15th century), stretching from the Baltic to the Black Sea
Occupied by the Soviet Union 1940–1991; the first Soviet republic to declare independence (March 11, 1990), triggering a 16-month standoff before the USSR recognized it

Small landlocked country bordering Belgium, France, and Germany; capital is Luxembourg City
One of the world's wealthiest countries per capita, driven by financial services, steel (historically), and EU institutions — the European Court of Justice and Court of Auditors are based here
The only remaining Grand Duchy in the world, ruled by Grand Duke Henri

Archipelago of islands in the central Mediterranean south of Sicily; capital is Valletta, the smallest EU capital
Strategically vital throughout history — held by the Knights of St. John (1530–1798), then Britain (1800–1964); awarded the George Cross by Britain for its WWII resistance
The Maltese Falcon (novel and films) was named for a legendary tribute from Malta to Spain; Maltese is the only Semitic language written in the Latin alphabet

Landlocked country between Romania and Ukraine; capital is Chișinău
The poorest country in Europe by GDP per capita; the breakaway region of Transnistria (backed by Russia) has been outside Moldovan control since 1990
Applied for EU membership in 2022 following Russia's invasion of Ukraine; historically part of Romania until WWII

City-state on the French Riviera; the second smallest country in the world by area (~2 km²), after Vatican City
Known for its casino (the Monte Carlo Casino), Formula 1 Grand Prix street race, and one of the world's highest densities of millionaires
A constitutional monarchy ruled by the Grimaldi family since 1297 — one of Europe's oldest royal dynasties

Small country on the Adriatic coast of the western Balkans; capital is Podgorica
One of Europe's newest states — declared independence from the State of Serbia and Montenegro in 2006
The name means 'Black Mountain' in Italian (from Monte Negro); joined NATO in 2017 and is an EU candidate

Low-lying country in Northwestern Europe; constitutional capital is Amsterdam, but the government sits in The Hague (Den Haag)
About 26% of the country lies below sea level, reclaimed from the sea via an extensive system of dikes, polders, and pumping stations
Dutch Golden Age (17th century) produced Rembrandt, Vermeer, and a global trading empire via the Dutch East India Company (VOC), the world's first publicly traded company

Landlocked country in the southern Balkans; capital is Skopje
A 27-year naming dispute with Greece was resolved in 2019 — the country was formerly called FYROM (Former Yugoslav Republic of Macedonia); joined NATO in 2020
Alexander the Great was born in Pella, in the ancient kingdom of Macedon, which corresponds to modern northern Greece — not North Macedonia itself, a common misconception

Located on the Scandinavian Peninsula in Northern Europe; capital is Oslo
The Nobel Peace Prize is awarded in Oslo each year (all other Nobel Prizes are awarded in Stockholm)
One of the world's wealthiest countries per capita, with the world's largest sovereign wealth fund (~$1.7 trillion), built from North Sea oil revenues
Known for fjords — long, deep inlets carved by glaciers; the Sognefjord is the world's deepest at 1,308 m

Located in Central Europe; capital is Warsaw (Warszawa)
Warsaw was 85% destroyed in WWII; the Old Town was meticulously rebuilt and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site
The Solidarity trade union movement (1980), led by Lech Wałęsa, was the first independent trade union in the Soviet bloc and helped bring down communist rule
Birthplace of Copernicus, Marie Curie, Frédéric Chopin, and Pope John Paul II

Located on the western edge of the Iberian Peninsula; capital is Lisbon
In the 15th–16th centuries, Portugal was the first European power to establish a global maritime empire, with Vasco da Gama reaching India (1498) and Pedro Álvares Cabral claiming Brazil (1500)
Portuguese is the world's sixth most spoken language (~260 million native speakers), largely due to Brazil

Located in southeastern Europe on the Black Sea; capital is Bucharest
Home to Transylvania — historically part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire and the setting of Bram Stoker's Dracula (loosely based on Vlad the Impaler, a 15th-century Wallachian prince)
Nicolas Ceaușescu's communist dictatorship (1965–1989) ended with his public execution on Christmas Day 1989 — one of the most dramatic falls of the Soviet era

Largest country on Earth by area (~17.1 million km²), spanning 11 time zones; capital is Moscow, the largest city in Europe
Transcontinental — spans both Europe and Asia; the Ural Mountains mark the traditional boundary between European and Asian Russia
The Soviet Union (USSR, 1922–1991) was centered on Russia and was a global superpower rivaling the United States during the Cold War
Has invaded Ukraine twice since 2014, including a full-scale invasion in February 2022

Microstate entirely surrounded by Italy; capital is San Marino City, the highest capital in Europe at 749 m
Claims to be the world's oldest republic, founded in 301 CE by a stonemason named Marinus; its constitution dates to 1600
One of only three countries entirely surrounded by a single other country (along with Lesotho and Vatican City)

Landlocked country in the central Balkans; capital is Belgrade
The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo in 1914 (by Bosnian Serb Gavrilo Princip) triggered WWI
Kosovo declared independence from Serbia in 2008 — recognized by ~100 countries but not Serbia or Russia; Novak Djokovic, the most successful men's tennis player in history, is Serbian

Landlocked country in Central Europe; capital is Bratislava
Formed in 1993 when Czechoslovakia peacefully split — the 'Velvet Divorce'; Bratislava is unusual in being a capital city that borders two other countries (Austria and Hungary)
Home to the highest density of castles in Europe; the Carpathian Mountains run through the country

Small country in Central Europe bordering Austria, Italy, Hungary, and Croatia; capital is Ljubljana
One of the most prosperous former Yugoslav republics; declared independence in 1991 after a brief 10-day war
Home to Postojna Cave, one of the largest cave systems in Europe, and Lake Bled with its iconic island church; joined the EU and NATO in 2004

Located on the Iberian Peninsula in southwestern Europe; capital is Madrid
Second largest country in the EU by area; built a vast global empire in the Americas following Columbus's 1492 voyage — Spanish is now the world's second most spoken native language
Antonío Gaudí's Sagrada Família in Barcelona (begun 1882, still under construction) and the Alhambra palace in Granada are its most visited monuments

Largest of the Scandinavian countries; capital is Stockholm, built on 14 islands
The Nobel Prize ceremonies are held in Stockholm each December (the Peace Prize is awarded in Oslo)
Neutral in both World Wars; home to IKEA, H&M, Spotify, and Volvo; consistently ranks among the world's happiest and most gender-equal countries

Landlocked country in Central Europe; technically has no official capital — Bern is the 'federal city' where the government sits
Permanently neutral since the Congress of Vienna (1815); Geneva hosts the UN European headquarters, the Red Cross (founded there by Henri Dunant in 1863), and numerous other international organizations
Albert Einstein developed his special theory of relativity while working at the patent office in Bern (1905)

Largest country entirely within Europe; capital is Kyiv (not 'Kiev' — the Ukrainian spelling became standard internationally after 2018)
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine in 1986 while it was part of the Soviet Union
Russia launched a full-scale invasion in February 2022; the ongoing war has been the largest military conflict in Europe since WWII

Island nation comprising England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland; capital is London, the largest city in Western Europe
Built the largest empire in history — at its peak (c. 1920), the British Empire covered ~24% of Earth's land surface
Left the European Union in 2020 ('Brexit') following a 2016 referendum; the Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the 18th century
Home to the world's oldest national anthem still in use ('God Save the King/Queen')

The smallest country in the world by both area (~0.44 km²) and population (~800); located within Rome
The seat of the Roman Catholic Church and residence of the Pope; established as an independent state by the Lateran Treaty with Italy in 1929
St. Peter's Basilica and the Sistine Chapel (with Michelangelo's ceiling, painted 1508–1512) are inside its borders
